assumptions: The intragroup dividend receivable/payable is denominated in a currency different from at least one entity's functional currency.
The dividend has already been recognized as an intragroup receivable/payable, so there is a recorded monetary balance.
The question concerns hedge accounting in consolidated financial statements under IFRS 9 and, if relevant, IFRIC 16.
No facts are provided on whether the exposure relates to a foreign operation or whether a qualifying hedging instrument has already been designated.

approaches.label: No consolidated hedge for an ordinary intragroup dividend
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally be with an external party. A plain intragroup dividend exposure is not eligible unless it falls within a specific foreign-currency exception.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not document hedge accounting in consolidation unless a specific IFRS exception clearly applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to external-party exposures, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Fair value hedge of FX on intragroup monetary receivable
approaches.reasoning: A recognized receivable/payable can be a fair value hedged item, and IFRS 9 specifically allows the foreign-currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidation if the FX effects are not fully eliminated.
conditions: the dividend balance is a monetary item
the entities have different functional currencies so FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
only the FX risk component is designated
formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met at inception
approaches.practical_implication: Document the FX risk of the booked intragroup receivable/payable as the hedged risk in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements.
Paragraphs 6.5.2(a), 6.3.7 and 6.4.1 support fair value hedge designation of a recognized item or risk component, subject to full hedge documentation.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of FX on intragroup monetary receivable
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows a cash flow hedge for variability in cash flows of a recognized asset or liability attributable to a particular risk. For a foreign-currency dividend receivable, the same intragroup monetary-item exception and hedge qualification rules must be met.
conditions: the dividend balance is a recognized monetary item
FX-driven variability in settlement cash flows could affect consolidated profit or loss
formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met at inception
the hedge ratio and hedging instrument are documented
approaches.practical_implication: Document FX-driven variability in the functional-currency settlement amount as the hedged risk.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2(b) allows a cash flow hedge for a particular risk associated with a recognized asset or liability if it could affect profit or loss.
Paragraphs 6.3.6 and 6.4.1 make this possible only if the intragroup monetary-item FX exception and hedge qualification criteria are satisfied.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of forecast intragroup dividend
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a narrow exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions, but the stated fact pattern is no longer forecast because the dividend has already been recognized as a receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not use a forecast-transaction hedge model for the current booked receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions only if foreign-currency risk will affect consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge of a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: This is relevant only if the economic exposure being hedged is the group's net investment in a foreign operation, not merely the intragroup dividend receivable. IFRIC 16 adds group-level constraints on the designated risk and where the hedging instrument may be held.
conditions: the exposure relates to net assets of a foreign operation included in consolidated financial statements
the hedged risk is between the foreign operation's functional currency and a parent entity's functional currency
the same net investment risk is hedge-accounted only once in the ultimate consolidated financial statements
formal designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: If used, the documentation must target the net investment exposure rather than the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge accounting model, subject to paragraph 6.4.1.
Paragraphs 10–15 and 13–14 restrict the hedged risk, prevent duplicate hedge accounting for the same net investment risk, and permit the hedging instrument to be held anywhere in the group.
